key_insight: Constrain adaptation to a trusted low-dimensional adapter subspace so fine-tuning can learn useful task changes while making poisoned or out-of-distribution updates hard to fit.
engineering_relevance: This offers a practical robustness mechanism for parameter-efficient fine-tuning in safety-critical models, trading some flexibility for stronger resistance to targeted corruption and backdoors.
method: Build a shared subspace from 196 trusted public LoRA adapters on flan-t5-large.
Restrict gradient-based adaptation to 128 coordinates in that subspace instead of full LoRA updates.
Compare clean-task performance, label-inversion poisoning, and adaptive backdoor attacks against standard LoRA fine-tuning.
evidence_signal: On the evaluated tasks, the constrained learner matched clean LoRA performance, kept 62–96% exact match under targeted label inversion where LoRA dropped to 3–26%, separated clean from corrupted data by about 120× in adaptation loss, and blocked an adaptive backdoor attack at 8% success versus 100% for LoRA on one task.
watch_item: The protection depends on trusting the adapter pool and can weaken when the target behavior lies outside the pool's covered directions, where unconstrained fine-tuning may perform better.
paper_title: Learning Only What Valid Adapters Can Express: Subspace-Constrained Adaptation Against Fine-Tuning Poisoning
